question: How to brew compost tea?
title: How to Brew Compost Tea: A Simple Method to Multiply Your Soil Biology | Scotty's Gardening Lab
metaDescription: Compost tea is a microbial-rich liquid brewed from finished compost and water. Here's how to make it at home with an air pump, and how to use it in your garden.
content:
I started making compost tea because I read about it in a book. That's how a lot of things in this gardening journey start for me — I read about some practice that sounds almost too good to be true, and then I try it and discover the book wasn't exaggerating.

Compost tea, when made correctly, is essentially a method of multiplying the microbial community that's already in your finished compost. You take a scoop of good living material — compost that's teeming with bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes — drop it in water, add some food for those organisms, and aerate it for 24 hours. What comes out the other end is a microbial-rich liquid you can apply directly to soil or to plant leaves. You're scaling up the biology.

The thing that makes this useful is simple arithmetic. A single gallon of compost tea brewed from half a cup of good compost can inoculate a much larger area of soil than you could cover by top-dressing that same compost. You're not diluting the biology — you're amplifying it. The aerobic conditions during brewing allow beneficial bacteria and fungi to multiply rapidly. You're making more organisms, not just spreading the same ones around in water.

---

**The short answer:** Brew compost tea by combining quality finished compost with dechlorinated water in a bucket, add a small amount of microbial food (molasses is the most common), and aerate with an aquarium air pump for 24 hours. Apply immediately — the beneficial organisms lose viability quickly without continued oxygenation. Use it to drench soil around plants or as a foliar spray. Do not let it sit longer than 36 hours, or anaerobic bacteria will take over.

---

## What's Actually Happening in the Bucket

Before I walk you through the steps, let me explain the biology. Because if you understand what's happening, you'll make better decisions throughout the process.

Finished compost is teeming with microorganisms — bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes — all of them in a relatively dormant or slow-active state because the composting process has consumed most of the available food. When you put a scoop of compost into warm water and start bubbling air through it, you're changing three things simultaneously: you're hydrating the organisms (water is essential for microbial movement and metabolism), you're providing a medium for them to multiply and move around in, and you're adding oxygen that aerobic organisms need to reproduce rapidly.

The molasses or other carbohydrate you add is food — a simple sugar that aerobic bacteria can metabolize quickly to fuel reproduction. In 24 hours under ideal conditions, a bacterial population can double many times. You're going from the relatively dormant microbial community in a scoop of compost to an actively growing, reproducing community many times larger.

KIS Organics, one of the more thorough practical resources on actively aerated compost tea (AACT), explains that the goal is to increase the diversity and biomass of beneficial aerobic microorganisms in the soil and on the leaf surface of the plant. That's the whole purpose. More diversity, more biomass, deployed in liquid form so it can penetrate the soil easily and reach plant roots.

## What You Need

This is one of the more accessible things in the biological gardening toolkit. You don't need expensive equipment.

**Compost.** This is the non-negotiable. Your tea is only as good as your compost. I use my own hot compost that's been fully cured — the worm-phase completed, good structure, that rich dark earthy smell. If your compost is immature or smells off, your tea will be off. The biology in the compost is the whole starting point.

**Water.** Tap water works but it needs to be dechlorinated first. Chlorine is specifically designed to kill bacteria, and you don't want it killing your beneficial organisms during brewing. The easiest method is to let tap water sit uncovered for a few hours — chlorine off-gases fairly quickly. Chloramine, which some municipal systems use instead, does not off-gas as easily and may require a dedicated dechlorination product. Rainwater or well water are ideal if you have access.

**An air pump and airstone.** A basic aquarium air pump with an airstone attached — the kind you'd use in a fish tank — is all you need for small batches up to about five gallons. The airstone distributes the bubbles evenly through the water. The goal is high dissolved oxygen throughout the liquid, not just surface aeration.

**A bucket.** Five-gallon buckets work well for garden-scale brewing. If you're covering a larger area, scale up proportionally — same ratios, larger vessel.

**Microbial food.** Unsulfured blackstrap molasses is the most widely used food source — about one to two tablespoons per five gallons. Some recipes also add liquid kelp or fish emulsion for additional mineral support and to promote fungal diversity. For most home garden applications, molasses alone is fine.

## The Process, Step by Step

Fill your bucket with dechlorinated water at approximately room temperature. Water that's too cold slows microbial reproduction. Water that's too warm — above 80 degrees Fahrenheit — can encourage the wrong organisms. Aim for 65 to 75 degrees.

Add your compost. About half a cup to one cup of finished compost per five gallons of water is a common ratio. I put mine in a mesh bag or old mesh onion bag so the compost doesn't clog any application equipment later. Drop the bag into the water.

Add your food source. One to two tablespoons of unsulfured blackstrap molasses per five gallons. Stir to dissolve.

Drop in the airstone, run the tubing up to your pump, and turn it on. You want vigorous aeration — the water should be actively moving and bubbling throughout. If you see dead spots where the water isn't circulating, add another airstone or reposition the one you have.

Brew for 24 hours. Brew time is important: 24 hours hits the sweet spot where beneficial aerobic organisms have had time to multiply significantly. KIS Organics and most AACT practitioners agree that beyond 36 hours, even in well-aerated systems, anaerobic bacteria begin to gain ground. Once anaerobes take over, you've shifted from beneficial to potentially harmful.

Apply immediately. Compost tea begins losing its microbial population within hours of the pump being turned off — the organisms need continuous oxygenation to stay viable. Don't brew it and then let it sit for days. Brew it, use it, same day.

## How to Apply It

Soil drench is the most common application method. Pour or pump the finished tea directly around the base of plants, concentrating it where you want to build root-zone biology. For beds you're trying to establish or rehabilitate, apply generously and let it soak in. The organisms will follow moisture down through the soil profile.

Foliar spray is the second major application. Diluted compost tea applied to leaf surfaces can introduce beneficial bacteria and fungi that compete with pathogens. This is the basis of the biological disease suppression claims made about compost tea. Spray in the early morning when temperatures are cooler and the tea will stay wet longer on the leaves.

For garden beds I'm establishing or top-dressing, I'll apply compost tea the same week I'm putting down compost or mulch. The liquid biology gets the microbial community moving quickly, while the solid material provides structure and ongoing nutrition for the growing community.

Cured compost that I let my worms work through is my go-to starting material for tea. The worm-cured phase — after the hot phase — produces particularly fungal-rich material with a more diverse organism community. That diversity translates into a more diverse tea. Diverse biology in your soil means more resilient plants.

Gabe Brown's framework for living soil applies here: you're trying to build the web of life in the soil, not add a single input. Compost tea done right isn't a fertilizer. It's a biological inoculant — a way of establishing and extending the microbial community that makes your soil actually alive.

---

## Sources

1. KIS Organics — The Ultimate Compost Tea Guide: https://www.kisorganics.com/blogs/news/the-ultimate-compost-tea-guide
2. Mother Earth News — How to Make Aerated Compost Tea: https://www.motherearthnews.com/organic-gardening/gardening-techniques/aerated-compost-tea-zebz1307zsie/
3. Homestead and Chill — How to Make Compost Tea to Fertilize Your Garden (Aerated): https://homesteadandchill.com/actively-aerated-compost-tea/
4. Rural Sprout — How to Brew Aerated Compost Tea: https://www.ruralsprout.com/aerated-compost-tea/
5. Cornell University Composting Science — Compost Microorganisms: https://compost.css.cornell.edu/microorg.html

sources.title: How to Make Aerated Compost Tea
How to Make Compost Tea to Fertilize Your Garden (Aerated)
How to Brew Aerated Compost Tea

scottyQuotes: Compost tea is super easy to make. You just take a scoop of compost, good living soil — I took the earthworms out of it — and then you add some good water. I use rainwater. Bubble it up for 24 hours with an aquarium air pump. What you get is a microbial-rich solution.
I started making compost tea because I read about it in a book. And then I tried it and discovered the book wasn't exaggerating.
After the hot composting phase, I let the worms work through it. That cured, worm-worked material is my go-to for compost tea. The diversity in that material becomes the diversity in your tea.
